turns.content: I'm trying to understand the difference between using standard library types like String and the custom Fake trait from fake-rs when generating test data.

The key distinction is that std::string::String requires manual implementation of a provider or trait bounds, whereas fake-rs provides the Fake derive macro which automatically generates implementations for you. When you use fake-rs with a String field, it can leverage locale-specific providers to generate realistic strings rather than just random ASCII characters. This means instead of manually calling a function to create a string, the derived macro handles the complexity by selecting appropriate locale data from your custom providers.
Ah okay so if I derive Fake on my struct, does fake-rs automatically know how to handle all types inside it like String or Vec?

Yes, once you derive Fake on a struct, fake-rs recursively analyzes the fields and applies appropriate strategies for each type. For primitive types and common collections like Vec or HashMap, it uses built-in providers, while custom types require defining specific provider functions that match the trait bounds expected by the macro.
That makes sense! So I don't have to manually implement every single helper function for my data structures anymore?
Exactly. The derive macro handles the boilerplate for most standard Rust types, allowing you to focus on defining custom providers only for unique or complex data types specific to your application.
